#260 TC TRIPLE
Climate change is posited to be a frequent factor in mass extinctions by some scientists who speculate that minor climate shifts can trigger a catastrophic cascade of effects. Although this pattern is (i)__________ in that it confirms warnings about our own possibly dire future, it is also somehow (ii)__________ as it sets forth a context for today's turbulent weather as (iii)__________ in a natural, cyclical process.Climate change is posited to be a frequent factor in mass extinctions by some scientists who speculate that minor climate shifts can trigger a catastrophic cascade of effects. Although this pattern is (i)__________ in that it confirms warnings about our own possibly dire future, it is also somehow (ii)__________ as it sets forth a context for today's turbulent weather as (iii)__________ in a natural, cyclical process.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, E, I　alarming; reassuring; unexceptional
pattern
pattern: standard unit headword
#261 SE
During sleep the brain-wave patterns of dogs are similar to people's, and they exhibit the stages of electrical activity that are observed in humans, so there is nothing there to __________ the notion that dogs dream.During sleep the brain-wave patterns of dogs are similar to people's, and they exhibit the stages of electrical activity that are observed in humans, so there is nothing there to __________ the notion that dogs dream.
答案：C, F　undermine; contradict
pattern
pattern: standard unit headword
#262 TC TRIPLE
In his perceptive study of the emergence and evolution of modern urban planning in Paris, Nicholas Papayanis challenges the widespread view of Baron Georges-Eugene Haussmann as (i) __________ figure in modern conceptions of the city. Although Papayanis (ii) __________ the importance of Haussmann's accomplishments and public work projects in the creation of modern Paris, he nevertheless argues that the forms of city planning and approaches to urban space these works display are less (iii) __________ than has hitherto been thought.In his perceptive study of the emergence and evolution of modern urban planning in Paris, Nicholas Papayanis challenges the widespread view of Baron Georges-Eugene Haussmann as (i) __________ figure in modern conceptions of the city. Although Papayanis (ii) __________ the importance of Haussmann's accomplishments and public work projects in the creation of modern Paris, he nevertheless argues that the forms of city planning and approaches to urban space these works display are less (iii) __________ than has hitherto been thought.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, E, I　the seminal; concedes; original
concede
concede: standard unit headword
#263 TC DOUBLE
The lecturer (i) __________ to provide innovative insights into important issues, but her promise of originality was more than she delivered: most of her pronouncements were (ii) __________.The lecturer (i) __________ to provide innovative insights into important issues, but her promise of originality was more than she delivered: most of her pronouncements were (ii) __________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：A, D　undertook; platitudinous
promise
promise: standard unit headword
#264 SE
Although diabetic or extremely large dogs need specific diets, specialist pet foods may not be __________ for ordinary dogs: some veterinary nutritionists now suspect that grain-free diets promote canine heart disease.Although diabetic or extremely large dogs need specific diets, specialist pet foods may not be __________ for ordinary dogs: some veterinary nutritionists now suspect that grain-free diets promote canine heart disease.
答案：C, F　salubrious; optimal
suspect
suspect: standard unit headword
#265 TC DOUBLE
Some historians of science maintain that the conceptual gap between successive scientific paradigms is so (i) __________ that scientists adhering to the new paradigm find it quite (ii) __________ to understand what their predecessors could have been thinking.Some historians of science maintain that the conceptual gap between successive scientific paradigms is so (i) __________ that scientists adhering to the new paradigm find it quite (ii) __________ to understand what their predecessors could have been thinking.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：A, F　great; impossible
paradigm
paradigm: standard unit headword
#266 TC TRIPLE
During the past decade, there has been a quiet (i) __________ research into symbioses involving microorganisms: purely descriptive and speculative work has given way to investigations generating data that allow for (ii) __________ interpretation and thus (iii) __________ unfounded speculation.During the past decade, there has been a quiet (i) __________ research into symbioses involving microorganisms: purely descriptive and speculative work has given way to investigations generating data that allow for (ii) __________ interpretation and thus (iii) __________ unfounded speculation.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, D, G　change in; defensible; reduce the tendency toward
reduce
reduce: standard unit headword
#267 SE
While the politician promised that her speech would be full of fresh ideas, all she really offered was __________.While the politician promised that her speech would be full of fresh ideas, all she really offered was __________.
答案：B, D　bromides; platitudes
promise
promise: standard unit headword
#268 TC DOUBLE
Faced with a drastic loss of revenue, executives of local newspapers belong to two strategic camps: the (i) __________ and the (ii) __________. The former, trying to do more of the same but more cheaply, are doomed. Only the latter, embracing a fundamentally different way of doing business, will prosper in the face of unstoppable change.Faced with a drastic loss of revenue, executives of local newspapers belong to two strategic camps: the (i) __________ and the (ii) __________. The former, trying to do more of the same but more cheaply, are doomed. Only the latter, embracing a fundamentally different way of doing business, will prosper in the face of unstoppable change.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：A, D　economizers; visionaries
doom
doom: standard unit headword
#269 SE
Lions are __________: when necessary they will hunt large game, like buffalo and hippopotamus, but they're not too proud to eat insects or porcupines.Lions are __________: when necessary they will hunt large game, like buffalo and hippopotamus, but they're not too proud to eat insects or porcupines.
答案：B, F　adaptable; opportunists
adaptable
adaptable: standard unit headword
#270 TC TRIPLE
For decades scientists believed in the (i) __________ of long-term memories: they were unstable for a few hours but then became etched into the brain for good. Current research, however, suggests that recalling a memory causes it to revert temporarily to an insecure state in which the recollection can be (ii) __________. Thus, memory is much more (iii) __________ than scientists had previously thought it to be.For decades scientists believed in the (i) __________ of long-term memories: they were unstable for a few hours but then became etched into the brain for good. Current research, however, suggests that recalling a memory causes it to revert temporarily to an insecure state in which the recollection can be (ii) __________. Thus, memory is much more (iii) __________ than scientists had previously thought it to be.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, E, I　immutability; modified; fluid
cause
cause: standard unit headword
#271 TC DOUBLE
It is hard to know exactly what is causing a worldwide proliferation of jellyfish, but we can speculate. The fishing industry has (i) __________ populations of jellyfish predators, such as red tuna, swordfish, and sea turtles. And when small, plankton-eating fish, such as anchovies, are (ii) __________, jellyfish flourish, gorging on plankton and reproducing prolifically.It is hard to know exactly what is causing a worldwide proliferation of jellyfish, but we can speculate. The fishing industry has (i) __________ populations of jellyfish predators, such as red tuna, swordfish, and sea turtles. And when small, plankton-eating fish, such as anchovies, are (ii) __________, jellyfish flourish, gorging on plankton and reproducing prolifically.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：B, F　depleted; overharvested
cause
cause: standard unit headword
#272 SE
In noting that critical and popular opinions about Li's art coincided, Chang __________ the existence of an exception to her general theory of art criticism, which posits that critics' views do not intersect with those of the general public.In noting that critical and popular opinions about Li's art coincided, Chang __________ the existence of an exception to her general theory of art criticism, which posits that critics' views do not intersect with those of the general public.
答案：B, C　concede; acknowledge
coincideconcede
coincide: standard unit headword | concede: standard unit headword

If a figure such as the editor and writer Pauline Hopkins presents (i) __________ challenges to a would-be biographer because of her confounding periods of science, largely unknown personal life, and (ii) __________ papers, the problems posed by John Edward Bruce are of the opposite kind: he published (iii) __________ throughout his life, had an extensive correspondence, and left a huge trove of materials at his death.If a figure such as the editor and writer Pauline Hopkins presents (i) __________ challenges to a would-be biographer because of her confounding periods of science, largely unknown personal life, and (ii) __________ papers, the problems posed by John Edward Bruce are of the opposite kind: he published (iii) __________ throughout his life, had an extensive correspondence, and left a huge trove of materials at his death.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, E, G　colossal; meager; indefatigably
correspondence
correspondence: standard unit headword

Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：C, F　can achieve dominance throughout; endure

Anonymous sources have a tendency to (i) __________ the information content of news stories in which they are quoted. Most of these sources are (ii) __________ because they spin and leak selectively for political, personal, or institutional gain; all the balancing quotations from other sources can never remove their (iii) __________.Anonymous sources have a tendency to (i) __________ the information content of news stories in which they are quoted. Most of these sources are (ii) __________ because they spin and leak selectively for political, personal, or institutional gain; all the balancing quotations from other sources can never remove their (iii) __________.

Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, D, H　degrade; unreliable; taint

#276 SE
Scientists are a generally __________ lot, but few can match in the depths of her perverse and unmerited obscurity the twentieth-century mathematical genius Amalie Noether.Scientists are a generally __________ lot, but few can match in the depths of her perverse and unmerited obscurity the twentieth-century mathematical genius Amalie Noether.
答案：A, B　anonymous; unrecognized

#277 TC DOUBLE
Orchestra players are not always the best judges of conductors. Not surprisingly, when evaluating conductors they put a high premium on musicianship and (i) __________ and therefore typically gravitate toward conductors who are both technically competent and (ii) __________. But the ability to galvanize a hundred musicians into giving a great performance requires more than proficiency and affability.Orchestra players are not always the best judges of conductors. Not surprisingly, when evaluating conductors they put a high premium on musicianship and (i) __________ and therefore typically gravitate toward conductors who are both technically competent and (ii) __________. But the ability to galvanize a hundred musicians into giving a great performance requires more than proficiency and affability.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：A, E　collegiality; personally likable

#278 TC TRIPLE
Victories in intellectual battles in the human sciences are rarely (i) __________. While factionalism may persist, (ii) __________ also tend to occur, as reigning paradigms selectively (iii) __________ those revisionist contributions that offer greater analytical comprehension or enrich our range of empirical reference.Victories in intellectual battles in the human sciences are rarely (i) __________. While factionalism may persist, (ii) __________ also tend to occur, as reigning paradigms selectively (iii) __________ those revisionist contributions that offer greater analytical comprehension or enrich our range of empirical reference.

Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, F, G　total; accommodations; incorporate

Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3

答案：A, D　affecting; moving

#281 TC DOUBLE
Scientists now recognize that the human brain has remarkable plasticity and potential for restoring lost function. Following injuries to the brain that cause permanent but localized damage to brain tissue, neurological deficits can often be (i) __________ by special training. In other words, changes in the brain can be (ii) __________.Scientists now recognize that the human brain has remarkable plasticity and potential for restoring lost function. Following injuries to the brain that cause permanent but localized damage to brain tissue, neurological deficits can often be (i) __________ by special training. In other words, changes in the brain can be (ii) __________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：C, D　ameliorated; electively produced

#282 TC TRIPLE
Computers' triumph in chess had been engineered not by creating machines that (i)__________ human thought, as most experts in artificial intelligence had expected, but by perfecting machines that played like machines. The analogy with flight is (ii)__________: as long as people tried to fly by imitating birds, attaching wings to their arms and flapping madly, they were (iii)__________ to fail. Once engineering escaped the paradigm of the familiar, however, people were soon flying much faster than birds.Computers' triumph in chess had been engineered not by creating machines that (i)__________ human thought, as most experts in artificial intelligence had expected, but by perfecting machines that played like machines. The analogy with flight is (ii)__________: as long as people tried to fly by imitating birds, attaching wings to their arms and flapping madly, they were (iii)__________ to fail. Once engineering escaped the paradigm of the familiar, however, people were soon flying much faster than birds.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, E, I　aped; instructive; doomed
doomparadigm
doom: standard unit headword | paradigm: standard unit headword

Wescott produced only one short masterpiece, but a great piece of fiction of any length is a cause for __________, not regret.Wescott produced only one short masterpiece, but a great piece of fiction of any length is a cause for __________, not regret.
答案：B, F　celebration; delight

By focusing substantial attention on Franklin's achievements working on the molecular structure of coal and on virus research, Maddox (ii) __________ reducing Franklin's legacy to her involvement in the (admittedly central) scientific story of DNA.

答案：C, F　thwarted; frustrated

For instance, he deplores the Royal Shakespeare Company's eight-and-a-half hour version of Nicholas Nickelby, which many found (ii) __________.

#290 TC TRIPLE
According to linguists and cognitive scientists, the human mind specializes in producing analogies. Part of the reason cognitive scientists believe analogy is a (i) __________ cognitive process is that drawing analogies requires some sort of similarity mapping of one concept onto another, and similarity mapping is a component of the inductive thinking we depend on. Another argument for the (ii) __________ of analogical reasoning in human reasoning is that metaphors, which are linguistically embedded analogies, are (iii) __________ language.According to linguists and cognitive scientists, the human mind specializes in producing analogies. Part of the reason cognitive scientists believe analogy is a (i) __________ cognitive process is that drawing analogies requires some sort of similarity mapping of one concept onto another, and similarity mapping is a component of the inductive thinking we depend on. Another argument for the (ii) __________ of analogical reasoning in human reasoning is that metaphors, which are linguistically embedded analogies, are (iii) __________ language.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, E, G　fundamental; centrality; pervasive in
metaphor
metaphor: standard unit headword

While most Centaurs (minor planets) are thought to be __________, astronomers have observed patterns of brightening from the Centaur Chiron, as well as activity similar to that of a streaking comet.While most Centaurs (minor planets) are thought to be __________, astronomers have observed patterns of brightening from the Centaur Chiron, as well as activity similar to that of a streaking comet.
答案：B, F　quiescent; dormant

Folmer’s book on Edith Wharton seems far removed from recent trends in literary criticism; this need not to be a fault, except that, in its title and introduction, the book (i)__________ to be conversant with contemporary discourse in the field, but in its actual analysis of Wharton’s work, it is marked by a very (ii)__________ approach.Folmer’s book on Edith Wharton seems far removed from recent trends in literary criticism; this need not to be a fault, except that, in its title and introduction, the book (i)__________ to be conversant with contemporary discourse in the field, but in its actual analysis of Wharton’s work, it is marked by a very (ii)__________ approach.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：C, D　purports; old-fashioned

#293 TC TRIPLE
If companies were taxed based on the amount of their atmospheric emissions of pollutants, they could make their own decisions about how to alter their operations. Firms could compare the cost of paying taxes with the cost of preventing the emissions in the first place and would, in most cases, do a bit of both. Firms that can reduce pollution (i)__________ would reduce it quite (ii)__________, while those that can only do so at great cost would (iii)__________.If companies were taxed based on the amount of their atmospheric emissions of pollutants, they could make their own decisions about how to alter their operations. Firms could compare the cost of paying taxes with the cost of preventing the emissions in the first place and would, in most cases, do a bit of both. Firms that can reduce pollution (i)__________ would reduce it quite (ii)__________, while those that can only do so at great cost would (iii)__________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, F, G　cheaply; substantially; curtail less
reduce
reduce: standard unit headword

答案：B, D　economy; concision

The fantastic conceit of the film's plot is that global warming (i)__________ global freezing. After a chunk of Antarctic ice the size of Rhode Island suddenly melts, most of what follows--an instant ice age, for example--is impossible as science but not as metaphor. The data from the Greenland ice sheet show that, over the past hundred thousand years, temperatures have often (ii)__________--so often that it is our own relatively static experience of climate that should now be seen as (iii)__________.The fantastic conceit of the film's plot is that global warming (i)__________ global freezing. Blank 2

答案：C, D, H　produces; swung wildly; exceptional

Blank 2
Blank 3

Blank 2

Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3

答案：B, F　a hierarchical; a stratified

heterogeneous: standard unit headword
#307 TC DOUBLE
Folmer's book on Edith Wharton seems far removed from recent trends in literary criticism; this need not be a fault, except that, in its title and introduction, the book (i) __________ to be conversant with contemporary discourse in the field, but in its actual analysis of Wharton's work, it is marked by a very (ii) __________ approach.Folmer's book on Edith Wharton seems far removed from recent trends in literary criticism; this need not be a fault, except that, in its title and introduction, the book (i) __________ to be conversant with contemporary discourse in the field, but in its actual analysis of Wharton's work, it is marked by a very (ii) __________ approach.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：C, D　purports; old-fashioned
arcane
arcane: standard unit headword
#308 TC TRIPLE
According to Merrin, many law school curricula are (i) __________ the challenge of fostering an adequate ethical understanding among their students. By focusing exclusively on what lawyers can or cannot get away with, core legal ethics courses often inadvertently promote a (ii) __________ ethics, thus creating an (iii) __________ audience for efforts to instill an appropriately broad grasp of the ethical challenges lawyers face.According to Merrin, many law school curricula are (i) __________ the challenge of fostering an adequate ethical understanding among their students. By focusing exclusively on what lawyers can or cannot get away with, core legal ethics courses often inadvertently promote a (ii) __________ ethics, thus creating an (iii) __________ audience for efforts to instill an appropriately broad grasp of the ethical challenges lawyers face.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, E, I　unequal to; cynical sense of; unfavorable
legal
legal: standard unit headword
#309 SE
Since some contemporary western dietitians believe that the only function of food is to provide nourishment, these dietitians view an emphasis on the aesthetic dimension of the culinary art as __________.Since some contemporary western dietitians believe that the only function of food is to provide nourishment, these dietitians view an emphasis on the aesthetic dimension of the culinary art as __________.
答案：B, D　unwarranted; groundless
promise
promise: standard unit headword
#310 TC DOUBLE
The scientist's motivation for (i) __________ a ban on the food additive cannot be called (ii) __________. He himself had a patent for the substitution for the additive food.The scientist's motivation for (i) __________ a ban on the food additive cannot be called (ii) __________. He himself had a patent for the substitution for the additive food.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：A, D　advocating; altruistic
concede
concede: standard unit headword
#311 TC TRIPLE
Uchiyama Shudian, specializing in Japanese texts, including translations, was the finest bookstore of its kind in Shanghai in the 1930s; its owner, Uchiyama Kanzo, envisioned himself as something of a missionary for the cause of Sino-Japanese cultural relations, bringing Chinese and Japanese writers together for positive dialogue. (i) __________, the (ii) __________ Uchiyama's shop remained (iii) __________: tensions between China and Japan in the 1930s made it impossible to continue the kind of interaction that had been achieved through Uchiyama's efforts.Uchiyama Shudian, specializing in Japanese texts, including translations, was the finest bookstore of its kind in Shanghai in the 1930s; its owner, Uchiyama Kanzo, envisioned himself as something of a missionary for the cause of Sino-Japanese cultural relations, bringing Chinese and Japanese writers together for positive dialogue. (i) __________, the (ii) __________ Uchiyama's shop remained (iii) __________: tensions between China and Japan in the 1930s made it impossible to continue the kind of interaction that had been achieved through Uchiyama's efforts.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, E, I　Regrettably; cultural exchange in; an anomaly
cause
cause: standard unit headword
#312 SE
Harper Lee’s narration in To kill a Mocking Bird is __________, mixing an adult’s and a child’s perspective according to no logic other than the immediate exigencies of the plot.Harper Lee’s narration in To kill a Mocking Bird is __________, mixing an adult’s and a child’s perspective according to no logic other than the immediate exigencies of the plot.
答案：B, E　a hodgepodge; a patchwork
paradigm
paradigm: standard unit headword
#313 TC DOUBLE
Though McDonough discusses (i) __________ the filmmaker's aesthetic principles, it is the description of the (ii) __________, the very vulgarity of the director's films, rather than McDonough's learned discourses on the aesthetics of the film, that makes the book so entertaining.Though McDonough discusses (i) __________ the filmmaker's aesthetic principles, it is the description of the (ii) __________, the very vulgarity of the director's films, rather than McDonough's learned discourses on the aesthetics of the film, that makes the book so entertaining.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：C, F　adroitly; flagrant crassness
discretion
discretion: standard unit headword
#314 TC TRIPLE
When the introduction of a nonnative species into a habitat correlates with the decline of a species native to that habitat, observers may (i) __________ a causal relationship. However, there is little empirical evidence for a relationship between invasion and extinction, and it is not (ii) __________ for native and nonnative species to thrive after the nonnative species' introduction, because the habitat's conditions favor both. When native species do decline after nonnative species are introduced, the reason may be a human-caused habitat alteration that affects native and nonnative species (iii) __________.When the introduction of a nonnative species into a habitat correlates with the decline of a species native to that habitat, observers may (i) __________ a causal relationship. However, there is little empirical evidence for a relationship between invasion and extinction, and it is not (ii) __________ for native and nonnative species to thrive after the nonnative species' introduction, because the habitat's conditions favor both. When native species do decline after nonnative species are introduced, the reason may be a human-caused habitat alteration that affects native and nonnative species (iii) __________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, E, H　infer; unusual; differentially
cause
cause: standard unit headword
#315 SE
Because its previously __________ beliefs have become the core tenets of mainstream politics, the activist group disbanded; with no skeptics to persuade, its purpose evaporated.Because its previously __________ beliefs have become the core tenets of mainstream politics, the activist group disbanded; with no skeptics to persuade, its purpose evaporated.
答案：C, D　quixotic; idealistic
arcane
arcane: standard unit headword
#316 TC DOUBLE
The report is admittedly (i) __________: it is intended to suggest new lines of research rather than to deal with the subject in a (ii) __________ manner.The report is admittedly (i) __________: it is intended to suggest new lines of research rather than to deal with the subject in a (ii) __________ manner.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：A, F　sketchy; thorough
flippant
flippant: standard unit headword
#317 TC TRIPLE
Although often criticized by former allies for having abandoned or betrayed their common cause, Nguyen was actually remarkably (i) __________ over the years. Examination of her career shows that she never ceased to use the best data she could find to illuminate the issues. If the data undermined positions she had previously (ii) __________, she followed the data. If the data confirmed her earlier views, she clearly stated the fact. If the data were (iii) __________, she counseled caution.Although often criticized by former allies for having abandoned or betrayed their common cause, Nguyen was actually remarkably (i) __________ over the years. Examination of her career shows that she never ceased to use the best data she could find to illuminate the issues. If the data undermined positions she had previously (ii) __________, she followed the data. If the data confirmed her earlier views, she clearly stated the fact. If the data were (iii) __________, she counseled caution.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, D, H　consistent; espoused; insufficient
cause
cause: standard unit headword
#318 SE
Although in the mid-1970s nuclear power seemed poised for a still greater role in energy supply, in fact the __________ of its prestige had already begun.Although in the mid-1970s nuclear power seemed poised for a still greater role in energy supply, in fact the __________ of its prestige had already begun.
答案：B, E　waning; ebbing
vindication
vindication: standard unit headword
#319 TC DOUBLE
It was fine to be (i) __________ in the old days when papers were still not losing readers and ad revenue was not tough to come by, but many editors today are uneasy about bludgeoning their readers with the inherently (ii) __________ work of cartoonists.It was fine to be (i) __________ in the old days when papers were still not losing readers and ad revenue was not tough to come by, but many editors today are uneasy about bludgeoning their readers with the inherently (ii) __________ work of cartoonists.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：B, D　opinionated; unbalanced
profligate
profligate: standard unit headword
#320 TC TRIPLE
Unlike news reports, theater isn't expected to stick to the facts. Built on a sandy foundation of make-believe and (i) __________, the form, by nature, traffics in (ii) __________. Good documentary drama exploits its inherent paradox: creating artifice from verbatim texts, it uncovers truths by playing on the tension between what's real and what's (iii) __________.Unlike news reports, theater isn't expected to stick to the facts. Built on a sandy foundation of make-believe and (i) __________, the form, by nature, traffics in (ii) __________. Good documentary drama exploits its inherent paradox: creating artifice from verbatim texts, it uncovers truths by playing on the tension between what's real and what's (iii) __________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, D, H　pretense; illusion; invented
uncover
uncover: standard unit headword
#321 SE
Readers looking for another condemnation of private equity firms should look elsewhere, for this book is not __________ such firms.Readers looking for another condemnation of private equity firms should look elsewhere, for this book is not __________ such firms.
答案：A, B　a rant against; a diatribe on
vindication
vindication: standard unit headword
#322 TC DOUBLE
To the avid reader of E.O. Wilson, much of his most recent book Consilience: The Unity of Knowledge will be (i) __________, as the book represents the culmination of a life spent thinking about everything from the social lives of ants to the social lives of people. Nonetheless, new thoughts have been mixed in with the old to produce a book remarkable for its (ii) __________ and ambition.To the avid reader of E.O. Wilson, much of his most recent book Consilience: The Unity of Knowledge will be (i) __________, as the book represents the culmination of a life spent thinking about everything from the social lives of ants to the social lives of people. Nonetheless, new thoughts have been mixed in with the old to produce a book remarkable for its (ii) __________ and ambition.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：A, D　predictable; purview
contingency
contingency: standard unit headword
#323 TC TRIPLE
For scientists who wish to conduct research in the national parks, the new plan promises a warm welcome in place of the (i) __________, or even resistance, that often greets requests for research permits. The plan says that "parks can and should be centers for broad scientific research and inquiry." It also maintains that scientific research should be (ii) __________ in parks where it can be performed without (iii) __________ other park values.For scientists who wish to conduct research in the national parks, the new plan promises a warm welcome in place of the (i) __________, or even resistance, that often greets requests for research permits. The plan says that "parks can and should be centers for broad scientific research and inquiry." It also maintains that scientific research should be (ii) __________ in parks where it can be performed without (iii) __________ other park values.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, F, H　indifference; facilitated; jeopardizing
promise
promise: standard unit headword
#324 SE
The controversial social analysis that Moynihan offered in the 1960s is now generally recognized as having been prescient; in fact, it has been the __________ upon which much of our discussion of social pathology must base.The controversial social analysis that Moynihan offered in the 1960s is now generally recognized as having been prescient; in fact, it has been the __________ upon which much of our discussion of social pathology must base.
答案：B, E　bedrock; foundation

concession: standard unit headword
#325 TC DOUBLE
This book, a more (i) __________ version of a highly technical report, is designed for the layperson, yet it is nothing if not (ii) __________: it grapples with very complex questions about the world economy.This book, a more (i) __________ version of a highly technical report, is designed for the layperson, yet it is nothing if not (ii) __________: it grapples with very complex questions about the world economy.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：A, D　accessible; ambitious
formidable
formidable: standard unit headword
#326 TC TRIPLE
Anna Barbauld's 1804 edition of Richardson's correspondence has been (i) __________. Although she (ii) __________ many of the letters that she included and made stylistic changes throughout, often for the worse, she was, by the standards of her time, quite (iii) __________ editor with regard to her respect for the original texts.Anna Barbauld's 1804 edition of Richardson's correspondence has been (i) __________. Although she (ii) __________ many of the letters that she included and made stylistic changes throughout, often for the worse, she was, by the standards of her time, quite (iii) __________ editor with regard to her respect for the original texts.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, F, G　unjustly disparaged; abridged; a scrupulous
correspondence
correspondence: standard unit headword
#327 SE
Although the company still loses the occasional lawsuit, the litigation threat that once seemed so __________ has become quite manageable.Although the company still loses the occasional lawsuit, the litigation threat that once seemed so __________ has become quite manageable.
答案：A, F　burdensome; onerous
promise
promise: standard unit headword
#328 TC DOUBLE
Goodin notes that people have (i) __________ cognitive capacities and that they therefore must consider some factors as (ii) __________ so as to be able to make decisions about other things.Goodin notes that people have (i) __________ cognitive capacities and that they therefore must consider some factors as (ii) __________ so as to be able to make decisions about other things.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：A, F　limited; given
adaptable
adaptable: standard unit headword
#329 TC TRIPLE
Letters present an awkward stumbling block to those who take a high line about literature as a custodian of moral values. The letters of accomplished poets and novelists are - as often as not - about drinking and infidelity, about sponging and wasting time. Consequently, one's (i) __________ the writer as (ii) __________ can often come in for a rude shock on contemplation of the writer's correspondence: the soul that composed the poems and the self that wrote the letters can seem (iii) __________.Letters present an awkward stumbling block to those who take a high line about literature as a custodian of moral values. The letters of accomplished poets and novelists are - as often as not - about drinking and infidelity, about sponging and wasting time. Consequently, one's (i) __________ the writer as (ii) __________ can often come in for a rude shock on contemplation of the writer's correspondence: the soul that composed the poems and the self that wrote the letters can seem (iii) __________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, E, G　reverence for; exemplary; irreconcilable
correspondence
correspondence: standard unit headword
#330 SE
Typefaces, in one sense, are just like styles of shoes: they __________ because different people have different tastes and identities and because both creators and users value novelty for its own sake.Typefaces, in one sense, are just like styles of shoes: they __________ because different people have different tastes and identities and because both creators and users value novelty for its own sake.
答案：C, D　proliferate; abound
coincide
coincide: standard unit headword
#331 TC DOUBLE
In China, beginning in the 1920s, the field of geography was (i) __________ discourse, alive to indigenous Chinese traditions even as it (ii) __________ key elements of Western scientific geography.In China, beginning in the 1920s, the field of geography was (i) __________ discourse, alive to indigenous Chinese traditions even as it (ii) __________ key elements of Western scientific geography.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：B, F　a hybrid; absorbed

arcane: standard unit headword
#332 TC TRIPLE
Why Zona Gale - a Pulitzer Prize-winning writer whose novels were both a critical and popular success - has fallen so far out of the literary canon is the fascinating subject of Deborah Lindsay Williams' recent book. Williams concludes that Gale's embrace of literary (i) __________ accounts for her eventual decline in reputation. Gale sought professional affiliation with other women writers of many stripes, and Williams contends that she has therefore been (ii) __________ by a later literary paradigm that favors (iii) __________ as a marker of artistic prowess.Why Zona Gale - a Pulitzer Prize-winning writer whose novels were both a critical and popular success - has fallen so far out of the literary canon is the fascinating subject of Deborah Lindsay Williams' recent book. Williams concludes that Gale's embrace of literary (i) __________ accounts for her eventual decline in reputation. Gale sought professional affiliation with other women writers of many stripes, and Williams contends that she has therefore been (ii) __________ by a later literary paradigm that favors (iii) __________ as a marker of artistic prowess.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, D, I　sisterhood; marginalized; individual exceptionalism
paradigm
paradigm: standard unit headword
#333 SE
As astronomers do not yet have a good understanding of the fundamental nature of dark matter, it should not be a surprise that astronomy students’ ideas about dark matter are, at best, __________.As astronomers do not yet have a good understanding of the fundamental nature of dark matter, it should not be a surprise that astronomy students’ ideas about dark matter are, at best, __________.
答案：A, B　superficial; sketchy
far-fetched
far-fetched: standard unit headword

Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：B, E　austere; largesse

#335 TC TRIPLE
While many tools can automatically locate patterns in large data sets, they cannot yet distinguish truly significant patterns from those that are (i) __________. Thus statisticians, who by virtue of their training are (ii) __________ identifying (iii) __________ interpretations of patterns in data, must increasingly bear the burden of scrutinizing data and challenging conclusions based on statistical flukes.While many tools can automatically locate patterns in large data sets, they cannot yet distinguish truly significant patterns from those that are (i) __________. Thus statisticians, who by virtue of their training are (ii) __________ identifying (iii) __________ interpretations of patterns in data, must increasingly bear the burden of scrutinizing data and challenging conclusions based on statistical flukes.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, E, G　merely fortuitous; adept at; spurious

There are many ways in which rat brains and human brains are __________; indeed, rat brains are often used as generalized models for all mammalian brains, including our own.There are many ways in which rat brains and human brains are __________; indeed, rat brains are often used as generalized models for all mammalian brains, including our own.
答案：B, D　analogous; comparable

adaptable: standard unit headword
#337 TC DOUBLE

Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：A, E　challenged; maintaining

concede: standard unit headword
#338 TC TRIPLE
Because popular culture often portrays lawyers as shady and scientists as eccentric, we might expect cinematic depictions of scientists employed as expert witnesses in court cases to reflect badly on both law and science. Yet science and scientists rarely (i) __________ in movies involving trials. When a scientist is negatively portrayed, it is often because the legal process is represented as having (ii) __________ science; when a scientist is positively portrayed, it is often because science is represented as superior to law, as (iii) __________ law's rhetorical and institutional instabilities.Because popular culture often portrays lawyers as shady and scientists as eccentric, we might expect cinematic depictions of scientists employed as expert witnesses in court cases to reflect badly on both law and science. Yet science and scientists rarely (i) __________ in movies involving trials. When a scientist is negatively portrayed, it is often because the legal process is represented as having (ii) __________ science; when a scientist is positively portrayed, it is often because science is represented as superior to law, as (iii) __________ law's rhetorical and institutional instabilities.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, D, G　suffer reputational losses; tainted; a counterweight to
legal
legal: standard unit headword
#339 SE
The recent exhibition on Dadaism is nothing if not __________, for the visual arts are currently awash in Dadaist gestures and gambits of one variety or another.The recent exhibition on Dadaism is nothing if not __________, for the visual arts are currently awash in Dadaist gestures and gambits of one variety or another.
答案：B, E　timely; opportune
sensational
sensational: standard unit headword
#340 TC DOUBLE
Whereas in the early nineteenth century phrenologists had seen every part of the brain surface as the seat of a specific intellectual or moral faculty, the 1830s and 1840s saw a (i) __________ of this belief, to such an extent that the brain came to be seen as (ii) __________.Whereas in the early nineteenth century phrenologists had seen every part of the brain surface as the seat of a specific intellectual or moral faculty, the 1830s and 1840s saw a (i) __________ of this belief, to such an extent that the brain came to be seen as (ii) __________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：C, E　reexamination; undifferentiated
vindication
vindication: standard unit headword
#341 TC TRIPLE
Because the term "memoir," unlike the term "autobiography," can suggest subjectivity rather than objectivity, Barton, in choosing to call his book a memoir, (i) __________ the accusation that his story may be (ii) __________. As Barton himself has noted, memoir promises only to give the reader an account of the author's memory, and no one but the author can therefore (iii) __________.Because the term "memoir," unlike the term "autobiography," can suggest subjectivity rather than objectivity, Barton, in choosing to call his book a memoir, (i) __________ the accusation that his story may be (ii) __________. As Barton himself has noted, memoir promises only to give the reader an account of the author's memory, and no one but the author can therefore (iii) __________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, D, H　deflects; untrue; gauge its veracity
promise
promise: standard unit headword
#342 SE
It is troubling that blogs, which may be among the least reliable sources of information in human history, occupy such __________.It is troubling that blogs, which may be among the least reliable sources of information in human history, occupy such __________.
答案：B, F　conspicuous; prominent
suspect
suspect: standard unit headword
#343 TC DOUBLE
After peaking around 3.5 million years ago, the number of large mammalian carnivore species declined gradually over the next million and a half years or so, mostly because the rate at which new species (i) __________ slowed down while the extinction rate (ii) __________.After peaking around 3.5 million years ago, the number of large mammalian carnivore species declined gradually over the next million and a half years or so, mostly because the rate at which new species (i) __________ slowed down while the extinction rate (ii) __________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：B, E　originated; remained largely unchanged
pattern
pattern: standard unit headword
#344 TC TRIPLE
The guarantee of academic freedom to professors is intended to encourage intellectual independence. But so long as faculty members receive training in a specific academic discipline and are appointed to positions defined by discipline, and so long as colleges and universities are organized by departments that (i) __________ long-standing disciplinary traditions, academic freedom will also (ii) __________ unique discipline-specific expectations. Yet the right to (iii) __________ disciplinary paradigms is also fundamental to academic freedom.The guarantee of academic freedom to professors is intended to encourage intellectual independence. But so long as faculty members receive training in a specific academic discipline and are appointed to positions defined by discipline, and so long as colleges and universities are organized by departments that (i) __________ long-standing disciplinary traditions, academic freedom will also (ii) __________ unique discipline-specific expectations. Yet the right to (iii) __________ disciplinary paradigms is also fundamental to academic freedom.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, D, G　inherit; tend to embody; resist
paradigm
paradigm: standard unit headword
#345 SE
In a time when so much new concert music failed to speak to listeners, the composer's symphonies expressed with __________ wit and scorching emotional power the tragic history he lived through.In a time when so much new concert music failed to speak to listeners, the composer's symphonies expressed with __________ wit and scorching emotional power the tragic history he lived through.
答案：D, F　caustic; stinging
patronize
patronize: standard unit headword
#346 TC DOUBLE
There is little question that in primates there is (i) __________ between monogamous mating partnerships and extensive male care of infants. Nevertheless, that does not mean male care is (ii) __________ situations in which females mate with more than one male; studies of savanna baboons show that male care certainly can occur in nonmonogamous systems.There is little question that in primates there is (i) __________ between monogamous mating partnerships and extensive male care of infants. Nevertheless, that does not mean male care is (ii) __________ situations in which females mate with more than one male; studies of savanna baboons show that male care certainly can occur in nonmonogamous systems.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：A, D　an association; precluded by
contingent
contingent: standard unit headword
#347 TC TRIPLE
It has been suggested that the use of metaphors from the realms of battle and sports to describe arguments contributes to the (i) __________ of the argumentative process. Such metaphors may (ii) __________ adversarial conceptions of argument, leading interlocutors to behave aggressively in verbal disputes, thereby (iii) __________ a process that ideally leads to resolution.It has been suggested that the use of metaphors from the realms of battle and sports to describe arguments contributes to the (i) __________ of the argumentative process. Such metaphors may (ii) __________ adversarial conceptions of argument, leading interlocutors to behave aggressively in verbal disputes, thereby (iii) __________ a process that ideally leads to resolution.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, F, I　breakdown; promote; undermining
metaphor
metaphor: standard unit headword
#348 TC DOUBLE
The study of the evolutionary interaction between herbivorous dinosaurs and the plants they fed on, as reflected in the fossil record, (i) __________. For this reason, many of the interpretations the author presents in her recent book on the subject are undoubtedly (ii) __________.The study of the evolutionary interaction between herbivorous dinosaurs and the plants they fed on, as reflected in the fossil record, (i) __________. For this reason, many of the interpretations the author presents in her recent book on the subject are undoubtedly (ii) __________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：A, E　is in its infancy; provisional

pattern: standard unit headword

Ultimately, according to Zuger, all medical advice is __________: it is educated guesswork, perhaps, but guesswork all the same.Ultimately, according to Zuger, all medical advice is __________: it is educated guesswork, perhaps, but guesswork all the same.
答案：B, E　speculative; conjectural
salient
salient: standard unit headword
#350 TC TRIPLE
Some critics say that the prime minister (i) __________ her positions too easily, but her supporters claim that she is merely (ii) __________ rather than quixotic and adaptable rather than (iii) __________.Some critics say that the prime minister (i) __________ her positions too easily, but her supporters claim that she is merely (ii) __________ rather than quixotic and adaptable rather than (iii) __________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, D, H　changes; pragmatic; capricious

#351 TC DOUBLE
Although fears of a dark, robot-controlled future are evident in the popular imagination, some scientists are more (i) __________ about the impact that artificial intelligence will have in real life. They share a generally (ii) __________ vision of a future in which robots interact cooperatively with people in a wide variety of settings.Although fears of a dark, robot-controlled future are evident in the popular imagination, some scientists are more (i) __________ about the impact that artificial intelligence will have in real life. They share a generally (ii) __________ vision of a future in which robots interact cooperatively with people in a wide variety of settings.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：B, E　sanguine; propitious
vociferous
vociferous: standard unit headword
#352 SE
Karin Knorr-Cetina argues that the production of technological knowledge is fundamentally __________: technological innovations arise within an "epistemic culture," that is, practitioners bonded by affinity who create and warrant emerging ideas.Karin Knorr-Cetina argues that the production of technological knowledge is fundamentally __________: technological innovations arise within an "epistemic culture," that is, practitioners bonded by affinity who create and warrant emerging ideas.
答案：B, F　collective; social
contingent
contingent: standard unit headword
#353 TC TRIPLE
Philosophy is generally considered (i) __________ business in that it is pursued by thinkers constructing and evaluating arguments according to dispassionate considerations of soundness. In this view, the flesh-and-blood men and women involved--their personalities and histories--are (ii) __________ their philosophical claims. Yet some mavericks argue that philosophical positions are (iii) __________ the individuals who hold them and are enriched by biographical information.Philosophy is generally considered (i) __________ business in that it is pursued by thinkers constructing and evaluating arguments according to dispassionate considerations of soundness. In this view, the flesh-and-blood men and women involved--their personalities and histories--are (ii) __________ their philosophical claims. Yet some mavericks argue that philosophical positions are (iii) __________ the individuals who hold them and are enriched by biographical information.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, D, H　an impersonal; irrelevant to; inseparable from
dispassionate
dispassionate: standard unit headword
#354 TC DOUBLE

Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：C, D　poisonous; naturally occurs in
cause
cause: standard unit headword

Hugh Trevor-Roper's series of essays on the Puritan Revolution are considered to be the __________ of the genre: though these essays have exerted a welcome influence on the work of many subsequent historians, their quality has never been equaled.Hugh Trevor-Roper's series of essays on the Puritan Revolution are considered to be the __________ of the genre: though these essays have exerted a welcome influence on the work of many subsequent historians, their quality has never been equaled.
答案：B, D　apogee; acme

Consequently, these treatments are widely used.For almost two centuries, the German island of Sylt has offered various therapies for every conceivable (i)__________, from broken bones to liver complaints. The local mud, saltwater, thermal pools, and spas have been deemed (ii)__________ by the German medical system, which (iii)__________ some of these treatments. Consequently, these treatments are widely used.

Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, D, I　malady; healthful; funds

答案：A, E　a magnanimous; a generous

While Americans are portrayed as (ii) __________ in this version of the story, research reveals that most listeners knew the program was fictional.The 1938 "War of the Worlds" fiasco, in which millions of people supposedly believed that a fictional radio program about a Martian invasion was true, is held up as an example of the (i) __________ of the American public. While Americans are portrayed as (ii) __________ in this version of the story, research reveals that most listeners knew the program was fictional.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：C, E　credulity; gullible

#362 TC TRIPLE
Unlike most other serious journals, which drain money from their owners, the Review has long been (i)__________. But the formula is not without its imperfections, which have grown more pronounced in recent years. The publication has always been erudite and (ii)__________ but not always lively and readable. (iii)__________, accompanied by a certain aversion to risk taking, has pervaded its pages for a long time.Unlike most other serious journals, which drain money from their owners, the Review has long been (i)__________. But the formula is not without its imperfections, which have grown more pronounced in recent years. The publication has always been erudite and (ii)__________ but not always lively and readable. (iii)__________, accompanied by a certain aversion to risk taking, has pervaded its pages for a long time.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, D, I　lucrative; authoritative; A staleness

答案：A, E　elucidation; demystification

Blank 2

It is true that windmills could be built virtually everywhere, whereas water mills (ii)__________. However, water mill’s great capacity and reliability provided a better (iii)__________ the money required to build the mill.

If we are to avoid a calamitous loss of biodiversity, according to Golbe, we cannot simply let nature take its course. Ongoing conservation management is (ii)__________, as we have, in a sense, become nature, and the responsibilities that come with the role cannot be (iii)__________.Given our species' increasing numbers and appetites--which are reflected in and compounded by global climate change--even (i)__________ species are likely to become endangered within the foreseeable future. Even when they concur on what cheating means, faculty and students often assign different levels of severity to specific violations. These differences can serve as a major (iii)__________ the creation of a commonly accepted set of standards of integrity that are consistently applied to all academic work within the campus community.Campus-wide discussion on academic integrity can be (i)__________ by the fact that faculty and students tend to define cheating in (ii)__________ ways. Even when they concur on what cheating means, faculty and students often assign different levels of severity to specific violations. These differences can serve as a major (iii)__________ the creation of a commonly accepted set of standards of integrity that are consistently applied to all academic work within the campus community.

Blank 3
答案：B, D, I　expansion; distrust; grudging

There is something seductive about (i) __________ judgments in the arts. When a critic peremptorily announces that Titian's canvases are the greatest ever painted or that Mondrian and Malevich are the only truly radical artists of the twentieth century, we enjoy seeing all the confusions of taste swept aside, even though we may (ii) __________ reservations about the (iii) __________ nature of these opinions.There is something seductive about (i) __________ judgments in the arts. When a critic peremptorily announces that Titian's canvases are the greatest ever painted or that Mondrian and Malevich are the only truly radical artists of the twentieth century, we enjoy seeing all the confusions of taste swept aside, even though we may (ii) __________ reservations about the (iii) __________ nature of these opinions.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, E, G　absolutist; harbor; categorical

#391 TC TRIPLE
Models of the interior evolution of Venus suggest that the planet (i) __________ periods of relative (ii) __________ and periods of instability and rapid surface overturn. In other words, Venus may "freak out" occasionally, getting rid of its internal heat in great planetwide (iii) __________ of activity, rather than in the steadier cycling of lithospheric plates that occurs on Earth.Models of the interior evolution of Venus suggest that the planet (i) __________ periods of relative (ii) __________ and periods of instability and rapid surface overturn. In other words, Venus may "freak out" occasionally, getting rid of its internal heat in great planetwide (iii) __________ of activity, rather than in the steadier cycling of lithospheric plates that occurs on Earth.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, F, H　oscillates between; quiescence; paroxysms

#392 TC TRIPLE
Many writers are (i) __________ the term "unauthorized biography" because it sounds so (ii) __________. Admittedly, biography by its very nature is (iii) __________ a life--an intimate and possibly unflattering examination by the biographer, who burrows deeper and deeper to reveal the unseen and illuminate the unexpected--but unauthorized biographies can be a public service and a boon to future historians.Many writers are (i) __________ the term "unauthorized biography" because it sounds so (ii) __________. Admittedly, biography by its very nature is (iii) __________ a life--an intimate and possibly unflattering examination by the biographer, who burrows deeper and deeper to reveal the unseen and illuminate the unexpected--but unauthorized biographies can be a public service and a boon to future historians.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, E, I　uncomfortable with; nefarious; an invasion of

She thinks we can (ii) __________ this without (iii) __________ the more exalted concepts of inspiration, the "inner voice," and so on. She, too, believes in those things.Flaherty understands that biological theories of artistic creation may (i) __________ people who cherish the idea that art comes from something other than the action of neurotransmitters, and she spends much time trying to shepherd us through the difficulty of accepting that the mind is actually the brain, a physical organ. She thinks we can (ii) __________ this without (iii) __________ the more exalted concepts of inspiration, the "inner voice," and so on. She, too, believes in those things.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, F, G　dismay; comprehend; discarding

However, evidence of significant contact between the ancient Mediterranean and sub-Saharan Africa has been (ii) __________, rendering these exogenous explanations (iii) __________.

Kids will seek out like-minded companions, and spurn others who seem different. But when kept within reasonable bounds, this in-group (iii)__________ generally evolves into a more mature friendship pattern.In adolescence, (i)__________ interactions are crucial in forging a self-identity. To be sure, this process often plays out in (ii)__________ as a means of defining and shoring up the sense of self. Kids will seek out like-minded companions, and spurn others who seem different. But when kept within reasonable bounds, this in-group (iii)__________ generally evolves into a more mature friendship pattern.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, D, H　peer; cliquish social behavior; clustering

#396 TC TRIPLE
Industry-sponsored scientific research on chemical safety often (i)__________. Media reports regularly imply that industry support of scientific work is alone sufficient to (ii)__________ that research. Even though the source of funding has been determined to be a less significant cause of bias than other factors, industry support suffices, in the minds of many people, to (iii)__________ the credibility of scientific work.Industry-sponsored scientific research on chemical safety often (i)__________. Media reports regularly imply that industry support of scientific work is alone sufficient to (ii)__________ that research. Even though the source of funding has been determined to be a less significant cause of bias than other factors, industry support suffices, in the minds of many people, to (iii)__________ the credibility of scientific work.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, F, H　elicits skepticism; invalidate; vitiate
causeuncover
cause: standard unit headword | uncover: standard unit headword
#397 TC TRIPLE
It appears that the terrestrial biosphere has been absorbing a significant fraction of anthropogenic CO2, thereby slowing down the rate of global climate change. However, the (i) __________ of this (ii) __________ is not (iii) __________: if pushed too far, the biosphere might become a net source of CO2, thereby accelerating climate change and causing a positive feedback loop.It appears that the terrestrial biosphere has been absorbing a significant fraction of anthropogenic CO2, thereby slowing down the rate of global climate change. However, the (i) __________ of this (ii) __________ is not (iii) __________: if pushed too far, the biosphere might become a net source of CO2, thereby accelerating climate change and causing a positive feedback loop.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, F, I　continuation; compensatory mechanism; guaranteed
formidablecause
formidable: standard unit headword | cause: standard unit headword
